scope_note: Tier-1 diagnosis of the client's current stack. This report describes what was observed. It makes no autonomy or outcome promises. Any build or managed-operation work is a separate, later tier.

rubric.scale: 0-5 coherence score per diagnostic category (higher is more coherent).

levels.label: absent
levels.meaning: no shared spine observed for this dimension

levels.label: fragmented
levels.meaning: pieces exist but do not connect; frequent contradiction

levels.label: gaps
levels.meaning: partial structure with material blind spots

levels.label: partial
levels.meaning: a spine exists but is inconsistent or unowned

levels.label: mostly-coherent
levels.meaning: coherent with a small number of open edges

levels.label: coherent
levels.meaning: receipt-backed and consistent across the stack

rubric.overall_formula: overall = rounded mean of the seven category scores

categories.name: Stack Fragmentation
categories.observed: Fourteen tools were named across chat, three note apps, and two task trackers. The same customer record lives in at least four of them with no single source of truth.
evidence: two conflicting customer lists (CRM vs spreadsheet)
categories.recommended_step: Name one source of truth per data type and route the rest to it.

categories.name: Definition Drift
categories.observed: The word 'active client' resolves three different ways depending on who is asked. The same question to the assistant returns different answers on different days.
evidence: two divergent answers to the same prompt
categories.recommended_step: Extract the current working definitions, have the founder ratify them, and lock them as the shared vocabulary.

categories.name: Agent Theater
categories.observed: Two workflows are described as automated but a person re-checks and re-runs each one by hand. The automation reports success while the underlying step is redone manually.
evidence: screen recording of the manual re-run
success log with no linked output
categories.recommended_step: Retire the labels that claim automation until a step produces a checkable output on its own.

categories.name: Missing Receipts
categories.observed: Decisions and automated runs leave no stored proof. When a result is questioned there is nothing to inspect after the fact.
evidence: outcomes described only in chat prose
categories.recommended_step: Have each run write a small stored record (inputs, output pointer, timestamp) so results can be checked later.

categories.name: Dead Workflows
categories.observed: Six documented workflows exist; three have not run this quarter and one points at a tool that was removed. Nobody owns the map, so stale steps are still followed by new hires.
evidence: one step referencing a decommissioned tool
categories.recommended_step: Mark each workflow live, stale, or retired, and delete the ones that no longer run.

categories.name: Founder Overload
categories.observed: The founder is the routing layer for most decisions and the only person who can resolve a definition conflict. Progress stalls whenever the founder is unavailable.
evidence: queue that only the founder can clear
categories.recommended_step: Move the recurring decisions into written rules the team can apply without the founder in the loop.

categories.name: Missing Decision-Spine
categories.observed: There is no single place that says how a decision is made, who approves it, and where it is recorded. Ten competent fragments exist but they do not share a spine.
evidence: approvals happening in three separate channels
categories.recommended_step: Stand up one decision-spine document: the rule, the approver, and the record location for each recurring decision.
